pattern: Since the product only grows when the smallest factor grows, a min-heap that repeatedly pulls out the current smallest value, bumps it by one, and pushes it back spends every increment where it does the most good.
hints: Bumping the largest number in the array barely moves the product, but bumping the smallest number changes the ratio the most -- so always increment whichever value is currently smallest.
A min-heap lets you repeatedly find and update the smallest element in logarithmic time instead of rescanning the whole array after every increment.
Push all the numbers into a min-heap, then k times: pop the smallest, add one to it, and push it back. After all k increments, multiply everything in the heap together modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, one element absorbs all increments
statement example, increments spread across two elements
single element, k increments all go to it
single element already at the maximum allowed value
all zeros, increments must be spread evenly rather than piled on one
one zero among large numbers, that zero must be raised first
already-equal values, increments should still spread evenly
many equal small values with more increments than elements

stress.description: random arrays with k ranging up to the full constraint
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 100000)
    return [nums, k]
